pick.rationale: Purdue holds a significant analytical edge with elite offensive efficiency (rank 1-2) and superior KenPom/Torvik rankings (8th) compared to Miami's 30th/35th. The Boilermakers' experience in the Big Ten and tournament-tested roster under Matt Painter provide crucial advantages, though Miami's balanced profile and ACC pedigree make this a competitive matchup. Purdue's overall talent depth and offensive firepower should prove decisive in what projects to be a tight Round of 32 contest.

reasoningStep.title: Purdue vs Miami (FL) Round of 32 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Purdue is favored based on superior offensive efficiency and overall ratings, but Miami presents a credible challenge with balanced play.
evidence: Purdue ranks 8th in both KenPom and Torvik with elite offensive efficiency (rank 1-2), while Miami ranks 30th/35th with balanced efficiency (offense rank 34-35, defense rank 45).
Purdue's 26-8 record includes Big Ten tournament championship momentum, while Miami is 25-8 from the ACC with strong but less elite metrics.
No major injury concerns reported for either team entering this matchup, though Purdue's Trey Kaufman-Renn had a previous shoulder injury scare.
Miami's path included a 7-seed win over Missouri, showing they can compete, but Purdue's offensive firepower represents a significant step up in competition.
Historical 2 vs 7 matchups favor the higher seed, though Miami's ACC experience and balanced play make this a credible upset scenario.

result.text: ESPN analyst says Miami can challenge Purdue in NCAA tournament 2026 Skip to main content Home NCAAB NCAAW MLB Women's Sports NFL NBA NCAAF WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Olympics Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers Jay Bilas said Purdue's March Madness path was easy. What about Miami? Dan Rorabaugh USA TODAY NETWORK - Florida Show Caption Hide Caption Who’s ready to put on the glass slipper this March? Two underdog teams that could make a Cinderella run this March Madness are Saint Louis and Miami (Ohio). Bracketologists seemed to be writing off Miami after their fate was revealed in the 2026 NCAA men's basketball tournament . At least one ESPN analyst came to the Hurricanes' defense. The Canes received a No. 7 seed in the West Region and will face No. 10 Missouri in what's effectively a road game in their first-round matchup in St. Louis . If they win to continue their March Madness expedition, Miami would likely get No. 2 Purdue in the second round (unless 15-seed Queens University pulls off a massive upset). The CBS selection show didn't spend much time discussing UM and its prospects in the bracket, perhaps because their lead-in was the Boilermakers knocking off Michigan to win the Big Ten tournament championship. And it seemed ESPN's Bracketology show would go on the same route. Buy Miami NCAA tournament tickets Jay Bilas got the first shot at analyzing the West Region and while he thought top-seeded Arizona had a tough draw, he felt differently about the bottom half of the bracket. "I think Purdue has a pretty decent draw here," Bilas said. In fairness, he did go on to say "clearly Miami-Missouri, that winner's not going to be an easy second-round game" before expounding on the weaknesses of No. 3-seed Gonzaga and No. 6 BYU. But then former North Carolina guard Jay Williams stepped in to challenge the Duke alum. "I think Purdue will be OK, but Miami's a really good team," Williams said. "Miami's played well. Jai Lucas has done one heck of a job with this team, obviously Tre Donaldson is a guy who can knock down shots, you think about Shelton Henderson, a guy that can attack ..." "And (Malik) Reneau is a matchup problem," Seth Greenberg chimed in before Williams talked up Ernest Udeh Jr.'s abilities on both ends. "So yes, I do like Purdue, but that could be a good matchup against Trey Kaufman-Renn and how they like to dominate the boards," Williams concluded. Miami has had an excellent season under its first-year coach, going from 7-24 to 25-8 and reaching the ACC tournament semifinals. The Hurricanes beat North Carolina when both Caleb Wilson and Seth Trimble (big
